data.title: IXUS: Has The Makings Of An Ideal International Core Product
data.description:
Introduction to the iShares Core MSCI Total International Stock ETF

The iShares Core MSCI Total International Stock ETF (NASDAQ:IXUS), which is issued by BlackRock, Inc. under the ‘iShares’ brand, is a $50B fund (in terms of assets under management) that covers a wide pool (+4000) of international stocks outside the US. IXUS, which will complete 13 years on the bourses in October 2025, is priced at an expense ratio of 0.07% and offers a dividend yield of 2.82% at the current share price (IXUS’s distributions are made twice a year, in June and December).

What Does IXUS ETF Do?

The stocks that IXUS covers are based on the MSCI ACWI ex USA IMI Index [MAEUII], which the former pursues by way of representative sampling.

Representative sampling is a process through which an ETF only holds a subset of stocks from a certain index, which in aggregate, closely mimic the risk and return characteristics of the latter. Note that IXUS consists of over 4000 stocks, but MAEUII consists of over 6000 stocks.

Despite covering a narrower pool than MAEUII, we can infer that IXUS does a really good job of mimicking the former, as its tracking errors over different time durations (one, three, or five years) are very low at roughly 2%. In contrast, most ETFs witness tracking errors within a range of 7-9%. If you’re not familiar with the concept of tracking error, note that it is an integral tool in identifying how well a passively managed ETF is tracking its benchmark index. It basically tells you how different an ETF’s returns could be from the returns of its tracking index.

Now coming back to MAEUII, note that it is a very comprehensive global equity index, not just in terms of the number of stocks (+6000), but also in terms of the threshold of market cap that it covers (it covers large-, mid-, and small-cap stocks). Basically, in market-cap terms, it covers 99% of non-US global equities. Also note that MAEUII doesn’t just pick stocks from the non-US developed market [DM] pocket in isolation but also considers emerging markets [EM] as well. To elaborate, it fishes for stocks from 24 different EMs and 22 different DMs, so in effect you get stocks from almost 50 different international countries.

What Are The Main Characteristics Of IXUS’s Portfolio?

Even though IXUS is supposed to stand out for its wide reach (over 4000 global stocks), because it tracks an index that weights its holdings on the basis of their respective free-float market cap (the share price as a function of the number of shares available for trading), the portfolio ends up tilting towards the largest stocks in the globe. Giant-cap stocks account for almost half of IXUS’s portfolio, and including large-caps, they account for three-fourths. Meanwhile, small-cap exposure is quite minimal at less than 4%.

From a stylistic perspective, hybrid large-caps dominate (large-caps that are not prohibitive from a valuation perspective but also offer reasonable levels of sales and earnings growth), taking up almost one-third of the portfolio. After large-cap hybrid, large-cap value and large-cap growth have almost equal weights, taking up almost 25% of the portfolio in isolation. Broadly, this suggests from a stylistic angle this portfolio is quite well balanced, and investors don’t have to worry about missing out on any particular pocket.

From a sectoral angle, even though IXUS covers stocks from 11 different sectors, the top 50% of the portfolio is dominated by three cyclical sectors, particularly the financial sector.

From a regional perspective, Japanese stocks dominate this portfolio, taking up 15%; however, do also consider that there are quite a few Euro region nations (not including the UK), which in aggregate account for roughly 30% of the portfolio.

What Are The Chief Risks Associated With IXUS?

IXUS is denominated in USD, but its assets are denominated in non-USD currencies (mainly JPY, EUR, and GBP), making it vulnerable to potential dollar appreciation or foreign currency depreciation as it does not resort to hedging. That means, even if the foreign holdings of IXUS don’t move much and remain relatively flat over a period (say, one year), a potential appreciation of the USD vs. all these currencies (say, 5% on average) could end up damaging your returns by 5%

Even though “past performance is no guarantee of future results,” investors may also want to have some context on how poorly IXUS has underperformed US stocks, as well as global stocks (which also include US stocks), until now. These two counters are represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Vanguard Total World Stock ETF (VT).

Since it got listed in October 2012, IXUS has managed to see price appreciation of only around 70%; in contrast, VT (global stocks including US stocks) has outperformed by 2.6x, while SPY (US stocks) has fared even better, outperforming by over 5x!

Some may argue that looking at the price performance alone would be rather myopic, as it doesn’t take into account the degree of risk these products have faced. Well, to counter that point, one may also take note of the respective Sharpe ratios, be it over the long term (10 years) or the short term (three years).

For those not familiar with this metric, it brings into consideration the standard deviation (the degree of variation) of an ETF and then tells you how effectively that ETF has been able to outperform a risk-free asset. Higher is better, and it tells investors the level of excess returns that a product has delivered for the volatility it has experienced.

As you can see from the image below, IXUS has consistently lagged SPY and VT, making it a less preferable option from a risk-adjusted return perspective.

So, the broad takeaway from the paragraphs above is that pursuing portfolios that don’t cover any US stocks at all may not necessarily be the most sensible option. The US, after all, is widely seen as one of the chief hotbeds of innovation and technology and home to some of the most exciting and fastest-growing companies around. Not even having some exposure to this terrain (by way of a VT, as opposed to an SPY) could result in missing out on some exciting growth stories.

Another way that IXUS could be accused of not chasing growth-oriented territories is its relatively limited exposure to emerging markets (the EM to DM ratio for this portfolio works out to 30:70). Note that advanced economies or developed markets have a real GDP growth profile of 1-2% p.a. (even through FY 2026), but the emerging market GDP growth profile is a lot higher at the 4% levels.

Then, as noted earlier, from a sectoral angle, IXUS has the look of a cyclically themed portfolio with financial stocks accounting for the largest share. This also makes IXUS quite sensitive to the shifting monetary policy decisions of various central banks, particularly the ECB (European Central Bank) and the Bank of Japan (BOJ).

Who Is IXUS ETF For?

IXUS could serve as a low-risk, cheaply valued, non-concentrated core vehicle for those who already have sufficient US holdings and are now looking to branch out beyond the US and are looking for equity exposure from not just international developed markets but emerging markets as well. Not only does IXUS offer a wide reach of over 4000 stocks (which also include small caps, which a lot of foreign ETFs shun), but also an inherent stability (as exemplified by an annual turnover ratio of just 3%, which is roughly just one-tenth the level of churn seen among most ETFs).

IXUS also does not suffer from any strong concentration effects, with a top regional exposure of just 14% (towards Japan) and a top 10 holding weight of less than 10% (whereas with most ETFs, the top 10 stocks usually account for over 40% of their respective portfolios, making them dependent on just a handful of names, with most stocks just making up the numbers).

Note also that VXUS garners an A+ risk grade from Seeking Alpha, which makes it suitable for even conservative investors. Traditionally, the variation of returns associated with this product relative to its average returns (as measured by the standard deviation) is almost 200 bps lower than most ETFs. This facet also helps investors pursue long-term financial planning with greater confidence than a product with a very steep standard deviation profile.

IXUS would also appeal to value-conscious investors, as it can be picked up at a price-to-earnings discount of 33% to US stocks and a price-to-cash flow discount of 43% to US stocks.

It's also fair to say that even though IXUS would primarily suit long-term investors, the enormous liquidity that it sees every day (of over $300M in dollar value terms, which is over 200x more than what the median ETF sees) also makes it a relatively safe pick for those looking to make short-term tactical bets on the prospect of global markets.

Which Are The Close ETF Peers Of IXUS?

If not for IXUS, investors may also be interested in pursuing two other non-US international ETF peers that also fish for stocks from both developed markets and emerging markets. The ETFs in question are-

  • Vanguard Total International Stock ETF (VXUS), and
  • Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU)

Like IXUS, both these products are also passively managed and track certain indices. However, while these alternatives both track indices crafted by the British-based market index provider FTSE (VXUS tracks the FTSE Global All Cap ex US Index, whereas VEU tracks the FTSE All-World ex US Index), IXUS, as we’ve noted earlier, pursues an index created by the US-based index provider MSCI.

IXUS is the youngest of the three products and has the lowest AUM of the lot, but take a look at VXUS’s mammoth AUM of nearly $550b, despite only being listed around 1.5 years before our product in focus and nearly four years after VEU.

There doesn’t appear to be a great deal of difference in terms of which sector and which country these products tilt towards, but note that from a market cap perspective, both IXUS and VXUS consider small caps, whereas VEU shuns them. VXUS also offers the widest reach out of all three, covering over double the number of stocks as the other two.

All products are very stable, seeing annual churn of only 3% every year, but VEU has the edge when it comes to the cost profile, with an expense ratio of just 0.03%, which is not even half as much as what it costs to own IXUS.

From an annualized yield angle, there isn’t a lot to split these three products, but one key differentiator to note is that both the Vanguard products distribute every quarter, whereas IXUS only distributes twice a year.

Summary

IXUS comes across as a stable, less volatile, well-diversified, and cheaply valued core holding for those who are already well exposed to US stocks and want global exposure that also encompasses traditionally neglected terrains such as global small caps and emerging markets.

However, IXUS isn’t the most cost-efficient product in this space (even if its expense ratio is quite compelling by general ETF standards), and its lack of US exposure has traditionally proven to be quite problematic from a risk-adjusted return basis.

This article answers these three main questions about IXUS:

  • What are the key features of the IXUS portfolio?
  • Who is IXUS meant for, and what are the risks they could face?
  • What are some alternative ETFs to IXUS?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Carvana: Improving Operating Leverage Justifies Its Valuation Premium
data.description:
I was once very wrong on Carvana Co. (NYSE:CVNA). Back in December 2022, I thought Carvana would succumb to cash flow pressures. Not only did this not happen, but Carvana came out as a stronger business with a stellar growth profile. In September 2024, I published a Q&A session I had with my close friend and analyst Charaka Janitha, who correctly figured out in 2022 that Carvana was destined for a major comeback. Since upgrading CVNA to a "Hold" rating following my deep conversation with him, CVNA stock has gained another 150%. Today, trading at a forward P/E of ~70x, Carvana certainly seems expensive, especially when you compare this to some of its closest peers in the auto retail industry that are valued at forward P/E multiples of around 15x only.

After a thorough evaluation of Carvana's fundamentals, I believe its expensive forward P/E is deceptive. The company is valued more like a platform operator and less like an auto dealer, but for good reason, as I discuss in this analysis. A deeper dive into Carvana's operating leverage suggests the company is unlike any other auto dealer, which justifies its premium valuation. Even more importantly, I believe forward earnings multiples do not accurately represent the business and growth profile of Carvana. Based on the findings of this analysis, I am maintaining my "Hold" rating for CVNA despite its seemingly expensive valuation.

Operating Leverage Becomes Evident With Growing Per-Unit Profitability

Before I discuss the improving operating leverage of Carvana in more detail, I believe it is important to establish that Carvana is indeed benefiting from operating leverage. We can do this by understanding the evolution of the per-unit profitability of the company. Given that Carvana is already profitable and that the objective of this segment of the analysis is to discuss the operating leverage enjoyed by the company, I thought it best to calculate the per-unit operating profits reported by the company.

In case you are wondering why I am using retail units sold when Carvana's wholesale business is also contributing to operating income, that is mainly to be consistent with the management reporting (this is just an extension of gross profit per unit reported by Carvana) and to align my analysis with the long-term objectives of the company that revolve around its high-margin retail business.

Reporting period GAAP operating income Retail units sold GAAP operating income per unit
Q2 2025 $511 million 143,280 $3,566
Q2 2024 $259 million 101,440 $2,553

Source: Author's calculations based on company filings

In Q2, GAAP operating income per unit grew by a stellar ~40%, while retail unit sales grew by around 41%. This suggests Carvana is becoming more profitable with each unit it sells, as the growth in units sold does not seem to have led to a disproportionate increase in operating expenses.

The next step of this analysis is to understand how this important metric continues to improve as Carvana scales.

Substantial Operating Leverage Comes From A Fundamentally Differentiated Business Model

The first step to enjoying strong operating leverage is not sacrificing profitability for growth. Arguably, when I first covered Carvana back in late 2022, the company was a victim of following a growth-at-any-cost business strategy fueled by excessive hiring and prioritizing growth over tangible cash flows and GAAP profitability. However, since then, Carvana has actually come a long way. This is evident in the growing Gross Profit Per Unit reported by the company. In Q2, the company reported Gross Profit Per Unit of $7,426, a YoY improvement of just over 5%. Stable growth in GPU suggests the company continues to focus on profitable growth, not maximizing short-term revenue growth at the expense of profits. This has set a strong foundation for the company to enjoy operating leverage.

When we go down the income statement, we find that non-GAAP SG&A per unit decreased from $3,845 in Q2 2024 to $3,385 in Q2 2025.

Exhibit 1: SG&A Expense Per Unit (Q2 2024 vs. Q2 2025)

After a deeper analysis of Carvana's business model and another very informative discussion with my friend Charaka, I believe these efficiency gains are coming from a few fronts.

First, the company is enjoying fulfillment efficiency, meaning that it has become cheaper for the company to do the last-mile delivery of vehicles. This efficiency primarily comes from the integration of ADESA auction sites, which the company acquired in 2022. Carvana has been investing in adding reconditioning capabilities to these auction sites to turn these locations into inventory/delivery hubs for retail sales. These investments are already paying off, enabling the company to move cars closer to the final customer. This has meaningfully reduced the fulfillment costs for Carvana, directly boosting operating income. In its Q2 shareholder letter, Carvana management pointed out how this integration of ADESA assets has resulted in a 10% YoY reduction in outbound transport miles per unit sold.

Having more locations has allowed us to reduce inbound transport distances by 20% year-over-year. More locations paired with more cars in our system has reduced outbound transport miles by 10%. And the combination of lower miles, better scheduling systems, optimized vehicle routing, and better execution have driven customer delivery times down by 0.7 days year-over-year. This progress is just one example of how our business is continually improving and delivering better experiences as we get bigger.

This is just one driver behind Carvana's operating leverage. As my friend Charaka always reminds me, Carvana is not your traditional auto dealer. Carvana is more like a tech platform. Continued tech investments, therefore, are key to maintaining its competitive edge and driving profitability higher with scale. The company has been aggressively investing in both the front end and the back end of its platform. On the front end, these investments are aimed at providing a hassle-free, seamless experience to car buyers. On the back end, the focus has been on reducing the human oversight needed in the entire business process. As discussed by the company management on recent earnings calls, Carvana is using AI for several business functions, including customer service and document processing. Demonstrating the growing efficiency of these efforts, in Q2, Carvana reported a 23% YoY increase in sales per customer service advocate. While unconventional, this metric directly informs investors of the growing operating efficiency of the business.

The below table summarizes how Carvana's SG&A expense per unit has declined materially in the past couple of years, boosting operating income per unit.

Exhibit 2: SG&A Expense Per Retail Unit

When it comes to advertising costs, in Carvana's oldest customer cohorts, such as Atlanta, the per-unit cost has declined substantially over the past three years. This is actually a good indicator of how Carvana stands to grow its profitability in the long run with sufficient scale.

While not related to operating leverage, from a P/E perspective, we should see the company benefiting from its efforts to deleverage as it removes interest expenses from the income statement. In the first half of this year, the company spent $52 million to repurchase senior notes due in 2028. In addition, the company seems well-positioned to benefit from higher interest income as its cash pile grows, negating some of the impact of interest expenditure.

Keep An Eye On Subprime Lending

Carvana, a few years ago, generated most of its profits from its auto lending business. This was actually one of the reasons behind my bearish stance on the company back in 2022, as I thought the company would face a liquidity crisis as a result of subprime lending. In a research note published by In Practise in May 2025, their analysts highlighted how subprime lending led to higher loan asset yields for Carvana in 2022.

Exhibit 3: Carvana's Loan Economics In 2022

Commenting on their findings in 2022, In Practise wrote:

In 2022, we discussed how important subprime loan profitability is for CVNA. Given ~40% of originations are subprime which are ~50%+ more profitable than prime loans, a significant portion of Carvana's gross profit, and therefore group EBIT, is driven from subprime financing.

While the company has come a long way since then, subprime lending remains a feature of the business. Given the challenges faced by the auto industry today, I believe it is important for investors to keep a close eye on Carvana's loan book to identify a potential inflection point where its lending practices may hurt its profitability. This is one of the key risks investors have to keep an eye on.

In addition to this, investors should also keep an eye on the seemingly expensive valuation level of the company, as failure to maintain growth at an above-industry average level may trigger a market correction, even if it were to be temporary.

Takeaway

Carvana is not your average car dealer. The company is enjoying operating leverage as it scales, and its tech investments point to further operating efficiencies in the long run. Carvana seems to be on the right track to dominating the industry and should be valued like a tech platform more than an auto dealer at this point. Its expanding margin profile and its platform-like business support this thesis. That said, I believe its current market valuation is an accurate, fair representation of its growth prospects. For this reason, I have decided to maintain my "Hold" stance on CVNA stock today.

data.title: Making Money In Biotech Stocks
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

How to trade and invest in the biotech space with ROTY Biotech Community's Jonathan Faison (0:35). Making money in biotech (5:40). Managing sector risk (9:20). Geron - a stock he never thought he'd own (17:10).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Jonathan Faison from ROTY Biotech Community on Seeking Alpha. That's the investing group you run. Jonathan, welcome back to Investing Experts.

Jonathan Faison: Thank you, Rena. I appreciate being here.

RS: It's great to have you. I'd love to get a brief refresher from you on how you invest in the biotech space, what your portfolio may look like, a brief refresher on your strategy for those that missed or forgot your episode from last year, and then how you would encourage investors to think about the biotech space right now.

JF: So I started writing on Seeking Alpha in 2015, 2016. Definitely didn't think it would be turning into the community we have now, over close to 500 biotech investors. Whether it's analysts, doctors of various specialties, it's nice we all contribute our ideas together.

As well as far as the portfolio goes, I can speak to the current snapshot that it's around 85% commercial stage and 15% clinical.

In general, I like to say, I started investing in 2008, and I didn't have a background in biotech, so I had to make up with discipline what I lack in knowledge. And so that involves position sizing rules.

For example, the maximum I'm allowed to hold in a commercial stage company is 10% of the portfolio in that one company. And for clinical stage, the maximum is 5% of the portfolio. I tend to trade only a few times per month.

In general, I found less is more, so I trade two to four days per month. And the rest of the time, I'm writing down my ideas, doing my research, trying to figure out where I got it wrong.

So that's nice. It helps me a lot personally. One of my weaknesses is FOMO, fear of missing out, or overtrading, especially if I hit a rough patch. I'll overtrade to make the game try to make the money back.

And so, one reason we've had three pretty profitable years in a row, again, just compounding gains, trying to be boringly profitable, is because of that slowing down and focusing on quality.

So that's where we are now. And, again, looking at a multiyear time frame. So a lot of these companies are looking at where is the sales going to go 2025, '26, '27, as well as the pipeline, clinical momentum readouts, etcetera.

RS: That 500 members, I didn't realize it was so big. In terms of the feedback you're getting from subscribers, does that help you navigate additions or deletions to your strategy, or has it been the reverse? Have you stuck pretty steady with that vision and just incorporated new subscribers based on its success?

JF: Great question. Not to pick on anybody, but I think on X, what you see a lot of times on on social media, X/Twitter, where a lot of the biotech community is, sometimes people can mute, block people who disagree with them.

And I actually do the opposite. I really like it when people step all over my thesis, tell me where I got it wrong. And so that's an environment we try to have in chat in a respectful way.

There's guys on there that are way smarter than I'll ever be, guys who've worked with the FDA for decades on end or guys from different specialties.

So if I'm invested in a cancer company or dermatology or whatever, and a doctor is saying, hey. This is how I use it in my practice, Jonathan, and it's not the same as what you're saying in your article.

It's nice that we step on each other's toes. People tell me maybe where the stock valuation has gotten ahead of itself or they bring up picks that maybe I wasn't aware of.

I think every biotech investor, something that resonates with us is going through high periods where you're red hot and feel like nothing can touch you. You know, you feel untouchable.

And then you go through low periods where you're like, I don't got this. I can't keep going. I give up. And I can think of at least four times where you're in the fetal position. You're like, I can't do this anymore.

And then some people in the community who've been doing this for thirty years plus or other guys who who've been there and done that, they pick me up off the floor, tell me how I can improve, how to change my rules.

That's where we learned exercises like when an analyst taught me the portfolio cage match exercise where I take a current stock I own and I compare it to a stock on my radar that I'm thinking about buying and I compare the attributes, the future upside, the risk involved, the management team, etcetera, and the winner of this cage match.

Think of it like MMA or something. The winner is the one that stays in the portfolio.

Other guys helped me come up with my pre trade questions. So there are seven questions that I publish in each trade post that I have to answer before I'm allowed to make a trade.

So I think that's the big thing about the community is I try to contribute as much as possible, but it's obvious to anybody there that I get as much or probably more from them than they get from me in that sense.

And it's nice that we all try to help each other out. So when you're riding highs, it's really nice to have somebody bring you down, keep your ego in check. And when you're at lows, it's nice to have people to encourage you and and help you keep going. So it's a group effort.

You saw uniQure (QURE), the gene therapy stock for Huntington's disease, rise a crazy amount after the recent data and also being aligned with the FDA.

And while I'm not invested in it, what I love is how that brings a lot of generalists, a lot of people who normally wouldn't care about the sector.

Their eyes are there and they're like, wow. There's a cure or at least something that's helping these patients live a much longer, higher quality of life.

And I think the more types of clinical wins we have like that, whether in Alzheimer's or Parkinson's, which is near and dear to me, schizophrenia, etcetera, it's just this halo effect that brings more dollars, more investors back to biotech.

RS: And what are you seeing in the coming months, the coming years come out from that space?

JF: Oh, there's a lot of ways to, pardon the phrase, but skin a cat, in this case, make money in biotech.

There's people who focus on certain themes like gene editing. There's people who focus on dumpster diving. They'll find these companies that are trading at negative enterprise value, below the value of their cash.

There's been some pretty big binary winners of late where people are - especially specialists who are able to read the data ahead of time, the last round of data and infer to the probability of success of the phase two or phase three readout coming up.

And the people who get it right make a lot of money even though those are events where you can lose, let's say, 80% or 90% of your money if you're wrong. So you have to size your bets accordingly.

Imagine whatever percentage of your portfolio you're exposing to that biotech stock, imagine the worst case scenario of losing 80 or 90% of your money, and would you be okay with that and size accordingly.

In my case, again, I try to be boringly profitable, so I try to focus on themes where there's a lot of tailwinds.

In the rare disease space right now, there's a lot of evidence that the FDA is being very friendly in terms of regulatory endpoints, letting companies run single arm studies where appropriate because it's a lot harder and maybe unethical in some cases to do placebo controlled studies.

So rare disease is a great place for us to hunt into.

In my case, I always tell investors this is why you need to keep a trade log is to look at your losers, your winners, where are you winning and why, and try to replicate that, and where are you losing and why and try to avoid that.

In my case, my track record is pretty poor when it comes to preclinical or phase one biotech. So I learned the hard way.

For me, my sweet spot is working and focusing on late stage biotech phase two or three companies' clear path to market.

I don't like science projects where you're just throwing things at a wall, indications, hoping something sticks, BLA, filing stage, and a lot of commercial as well.

Another area that's near and dear to me is biotech companies on the cusp of becoming growth stocks. What I mean is a lot of these growth investors who are non biotech specialists, they typically steer clear of the biotech sector.

But when they do find a biotech company that already has the drug approved, so there's not the regulatory risk, the cash burn rate is reasonable.

It looks like there's a path to profitability or maybe they're already profitable or close to it.

And the sales are ramping up in a large market. Those are the sweet spots if you can see those, whether a few quarters or a couple years ahead of time and get into those. It's pretty fun as they become more broadly appealing to these investors outside of the sector.

A recent example was Arcutis (ARQT) that we entered around the sevens, and now it's around $20, I believe.

They had Zoriv, their cream for, it was approved for psoriasis first, then seb derm, then it was approved for atopic dermatitis, and then recently, or not recently, but more recently, scalp and body dermatitis. Now they're expanding to other indications as well.

And it's just one of those stories where the company is becoming profitable. The sales are ramping. We recently sold it for a nice profit to move into other undervalued setups, but that's been a blueprint from the playbook that we've been repeating a lot is trying to find these companies in the early stage of their growth cycle, but with as much of the risk off the table as possible, whether it's the drugs already approved or the phase three dataset is already highly compelling, things like that.

RS: What else would you say about the risk part of things? I know it's a risky sector. How else would you encourage investors to think about the risk part of things?

JF: You have to know yourself. I say in my twenties, I did a lot of swing trading in biotech, binaries, binary readouts, etcetera.

And you know, it's fun to wake up to a stock tripling on the data, and you feel like the man, but you also have to be okay with the fact that you're gonna wake up and if you got the readout wrong and let's say the company that doesn't have other assets or their assets are preclinical, so it's probably gonna trade not only at cash value, but all the way down to below cash.

So these companies, when that happens and there's a negative readout, they can lose 80 or 90% of their value.

And so the problem that I hate seeing out there is when, I hate to say retail investors because I am one too, but people who maybe who have less experience and they put way more of their portfolio where they go all in, heaven forbid, and they will wake up to one of those losses.

And that's why it's so important to be like, okay, I have 3% of my portfolio exposed to this company. So if it ends up being a 10 bagger, that's great. It'll become 30%. And if, worst case scenario, I lose all of it, that's gonna be only 3% of my portfolio that I lose, and I'm going to live and fight another day.

One fund manager put it as, your goal is to build as resilient a portfolio as possible and not only your portfolio, but your lifestyle so you'll be able to stand firm in the middle of any gut punches or negative events that come your way, and I think that goes double for biotech as far as managing risk.

RS: Anything else to say about recent trades or some of the names that you like or that you would avoid for that matter?

JF: I'll give an example with, Syndax Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:SNDX), which is still our top holding, I believe. It's risen since, I think we're sitting around a 60% gain, but we got in around $9.

And it was a simple story too where they have their lead menin inhibitor, Reviforage, which was approved in KMT two AML, a subset of AML patients. And they have fifty US rights partnered with Incyte (INCY) for their other drug in graft versus host disease, Nicktenbo. And it's already nice that both of those launches are fast out of the gate, doing quite well.

And the news came out recently in their Q2 report that the treatment duration for Revuforged was prior expected to be three to six months for these patients.

And the thesis strengthened because now the company is saying the duration of therapy will be six to twelve months, so much longer than anticipated.

And not only that, but a survey showed that most of the doctors after stem cell transplant intend to put their patients back on Revuforged, and that'll be even longer treatment time for the maintenance setting.

And so that's a great example of a thesis that has strengthened.

With that blank slate mentality, I look at the stock, the present risk reward, and that's why we haven't taken any partial profits because we're looking at it right now and where are the sales going 2026, 2027, as they expand into other indications.

They recently were listed in the NCCN guidelines for the MPM one, patient opportunity, which is even larger than KMT two a and that PDUFA date should be coming up. And so if they get formal approval there, then they have two indications and so on and so forth.

So it's funny. People will ask me what's the updated trade plan there, and majority of the time, from my end, it's holding patiently.

That's where the big money's made. But if valuation, if the stock goes up to 25 or 30 and valuation gets ahead of itself, that's typically where I at least take partial profits.

But again, there's quite a few setups out there like that that I think are still being overlooked.

Right now, it feels like a lot of investors and traders are chasing binaries, these smaller companies. And as a result, some of these commercial stage companies are actually in pretty attractive territory.

RS: Are you paying a lot of attention to what's happening in in terms of President Trump talking about various things that may affect the biotech sector, like the visa situation, price cuts in drugs. Is that something that you pay a lot of attention to?

JF: It's been nice in chat to have guys who actually have worked with the FDA and are able to provide more insight on that than I can, so I look to them.

But, in general, I try to, there's top down investors looking at the sector, the macro, etcetera, and I tend to be bottom up.

So I'm looking at each story and, where are the sales going, where is the pipeline going? So that tends to be a bit of a lesser concern for me.

Don't get me wrong. I love tailwinds for the sector. Hate headwinds. And it's harder to make money when you're swimming upstream. It is possible, but it's harder.

So it'd be nice to have these factors, including regulatory and legislative, and and, of course, the rhetoric coming from the president to be in our favor. It does sound like the administration wants to rack up more wins, especially in the rare disease space.

And so that's one thing that's nice too is seeing a lot of these companies able to design very efficient trials that burn less cash, get them to market faster. So I've been appreciative on that front.

RS: Anything that you're willing to share with us about what the former FDA players have shared with you?

JF: There's one guy, Osmium Research, who he worked at a medical device, and he's very, very, how do I put it pleasantly, he's very, very critical of the agency. He feels like sometimes it's easier to get a drug approved in Europe than it is in the US.

And he talks about examples he used, Iovance (IOVA) and a few others where whether it was an an assay or other other manufacturing concerns that he felt should have been, where a drug was given a complete response letter that should have been approved and maybe with observations of what needs to be fixed before launch.

So he's definitely very critical of drugs that should have been approved of the process, drugs that should have been approved that weren't where extra trials were required.

So I think one thing I learned from him on the whole is just not to underestimate regulatory risk.

Even something like a Kura Oncology (KURA), which we own now, that was a run up play for us. It's around 7% of the portfolio, KURA, but same thing.

They have PDUFA date coming up. They would be the second menin inhibitor approved for, in this case, MPM one, mutated AML. And same thing, you never know if there's gonna be some hidden surprise on manufacturing or you name it.

So, again, that's where proper portfolio waiting, position sizing takes place. You always plan for the unexpected just in case.

RS: The biotech space within small caps, people talk of rate cuts and how they benefit small caps.

We've seen a bit of a downtrend in that space. What would you say about biotechs within the small cap space? And maybe if you have anything to say about small caps in general right now.

JF: I'm definitely not a macro guy as far as having a crystal ball, but one thing that's kind of been obvious and talked about lately is a lot of the other sectors in small cap land or tech have been pretty overheated.

And even though biotech has been pretty frothy lately, if you zoom out and compare it to the 2021 (XBI) chart, I don't have it in front of me, but you're still down something like 40% from highs. And so I think there's gonna be a lot of this trade of going from areas of the market like AI, etcetera, that are overheated, and some people are moving those dollars to biotech saying, hey. This is gonna catch up.

So I can't tell you from my end crystal ball wise whether that's gonna continue happening, but it's been nice to be a beneficiary of that.

And, again, as we talked about earlier, biotech tends to move in cycles. So if we've had a a four year down cycle, it would make sense if we have a multiyear upswing as well.

RS: Anything else that you feel worthy of sharing with investors right now looking at the space or interested in the space?

JF: I'm trying to think of which names would be appropriate. And maybe an example would be Geron Corporation (NASDAQ:GERN) which is a company I never thought I would enter.

In the past, they had poor management. They delivered their shareholders into oblivion. And then I looked at the stock recently, listened to KOL calls, and you had the market capitalization of around 900,000,000.

And that puts what the enterprise value is getting closer to. I forget if it's around 500,000,000. They have Rytello, which is approved in MDS, blood cancer, and it's a very novel mechanism, a telomerase inhibitor.

And they have some gnarly side effect profile on the drug, but it's actually, most of it, the adverse events clear, in the early days or weeks of treatment. And what's nice is, it applies to, it works especially well on RS negative patients, which are two thirds of the overall population.

Even though there are other, better drugs out there that have been approved, that have moved to first line, these patients, whether there's ten percent of them getting this first line or most of them get it second or third, eventually, they're gonna get on this drug.

And so this is our bread and butter setup where you're buying a company, you're buying a stock at around the enterprise value is one times very conservative peak sales.

So peak sales for the drug is, let's say, 500,000,000. You're hopefully finding yourself in a situation here at around a 1.40 share price of heads win well you know, win big if the company, if sales pick up from here 2026, 2027. And tails, hopefully, the loss would be manageable.

And they have a readout coming up in myelofibrosis, enough that I don't give any credit to. And mostly all the competitors there have just tried to show a benefit on spleen volume and symptoms.

And they would be the first company trying to actually use ambitious overall survival endpoint, trying to show a benefit in overall survival. So it's riskier in that sense, but if and again, that's a big if caveat. They are able to prove that. That would be a big upside driver as well.

But, again, what's nice is we don't need that to work to do well on the stock.

So that's just an example of these companies we find at lows that are kind of thrown out and are discarded by investors or out of favor. The company had Q1 seasonality, I think, and also the sales launch out of the gate.

You wouldn't describe it as ugly, but it hasn't been a good launch either. It takes time to build physician awareness, and physicians need time to try the drug, get acquainted, get comfortable with the side effect profile.

And from what I've noticed in the trends with physician awareness, that's getting better over time, willingness to prescribe. And I think the bet is at least, currently 9% of the portfolio that that will translate into sales growth 2026 to 2027.

And full context, we're slightly in the red there. We're still only like 5% in the red there. Just so people know where the position is, full disclosure.

RS: Your investing group, once again, is ROTY Biotech Community. Happy for you to have the last words or share where else investors can get in touch with you or hear from you.

JF: The nice thing I like saying about these communities on Seeking Alpha and not just my own is, when I started investing, I had nobody to teach me, invest in my life savings, lost half of it, took out a big loan, lost most of that too. I learned everything the hard way.

So I paid for a lot of services too. And what's nice with ROTY Biotech community, there's a free trial for two weeks. So I love telling people, kick the tires for two weeks, copy my portfolio, my thesis for each holding, read our discussions and chat.

And, if times are tough or you're not wanting to spend for a service right now, completely get that. At least you're able to use those two weeks, copy anything that helps you, including my rules. I have not only my winners there, but also my worst losers posted and what I learned from them.

So, hopefully, you can learn from those versus doing them yourself. And that's what these services are there for, to help accelerate your learning curve versus doing it the the hard way like I did.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla: Great Company; Wrong Price
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) remains the most disruptive auto manufacturer, perhaps one of the most disruptive across the whole industrial technology sector. It transformed what an EV could be, built a global supply chain from scratch, and created a brand with cult-like dedication.

That is something that doesn't disappear just because the stock is overvalued.

The problem isn't whether Tesla will continue to grow; it's how much of that future is already factored in. With an EV/Sales of over 15 and a forward PE of close to 300, investors are paying luxury-tech multiples for an increasingly commodity-like, price-compete, heavy capex-intensive, and regulatory-risk-laden business around autonomy.

Tesla remains a fantastic company, and Elon Musk is brilliant, but it's not a great investment opportunity today.

Valuation

Tesla is seen as if it were a clean-play software platform and not a manufacturer with cyclical risk. Meanwhile, the profit margin and growth tell us otherwise.

Tesla's fundamentals remain solid, revenue has tripled since 2020, and free cash flow has increased handsomely from $2.8B to ~$5.6B (TTM). But growth isn't the problem with this stock; it's the price investors are paying for it.

Operating margins are where things get juicy. Tesla's profitability in 2022 reached 16.8%, when Tesla was riding the post-pandemic price wave and harvesting record EV demand.

Margins thereafter have compressed to only a bit over 6% (TTM), and free cash flow fell significantly from its peak back in 2022. Despite record-level revenues in 2024, Tesla is now generating roughly the same level of operating income that it was producing two years ago. That is, earnings growth has leveled off, but the valuation hasn't corrected.

At nearly 300× forward earnings and 15× EV/Sales, Tesla is not priced as an auto company but as a tech or robotics growth play.

But even in that space, it's at a premium: Nvidia (NVDA) is trading around 42× forward earnings after huge EPS growth. To put this into perspective, a stock that by many is considered as highly overvalued, namely Palantir Technologies (PLTR), is trading only slightly above Tesla on a P/E Non-GAAP basis.

ABB (OTCPK:ABBNY) and Fanuc (OTCPK:FANUY), incumbent players in the robotics market, quote between 4x EV/Sales and 4.3x EV/Sales.

Boston Dynamics' implied valuation from recent private funding rounds also lies below Tesla's multiple on an adjusted-for-growth basis.

In the automotive space, you have similar or higher margins on lower multiples. Even if Tesla's autonomy and robotics efforts pay off, the valuation already baked in today assumes flawless execution for years to come.

Below, you can find some other peers in the EV space that we can take a look at. For example, Rivian Automotive (RIVN), Ford (F), BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY), and Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY). Sure, we need to put a premium on Tesla for all the other stuff it does, but how big should that premium be? That's the big question.

The thing you can take away from this paragraph is pretty clear. Tesla's fundamentals are solid but no longer growing fast enough to justify the valuation. Investors still are paying a technology premium for a company whose margins increasingly look more like those of an automaker.

The Growth Story Is Changing

EV Competition is intensifying. BYD, Hyundai, and legacy automakers are closing the technology gap. China's price war and dumping practices are overflowing markets across the globe and causing margins to take a hit across the board.

Then we have the autonomy timeline, which continues to be moved. Full Self-Driving (FSD) is in beta, some nine years after launch. Regulatory hurdles and hardware tweaks limit near-term monetization.

Energy and Storage are promising but remain incomplete: Energy revenue is <10% of total and lower margin. Expanding it will be a matter of time, and there will be huge capex spend, more on that later.

Last but not least, we have the multiple compression. Tesla is leaving hyper-growth and entering large-cap growth. Even outstanding execution can't turn back multiple normalization.

What a lot of investors don't realize is that great business doesn't always mean great investment

I'm not a Tesla bear. The company is visionary, richly funded, and led by one of the greatest operators of all time. But as an investor, I care about return per unit of risk. Nonetheless, I would love to buy some xAI or SpaceX if it would be reasonably valued.

For context, some of the stocks I have written about like: Molina Healthcare (MOH) is at ~8× earnings with 19% revenue CAGR and 26% ROE. Sure, these are completely different stocks, but I believe these are better opportunities.

Crocs (CROX) is ~7× earnings with ~60% gross margins and good buybacks.

Sezzle (SEZL) is growing like crazy and is much cheaper than Tesla.

Both have concrete catalysts, solid cash flows, and fair valuations. Tesla, however, is offering massive uncertainty at 10x the multiple.

For Tesla, there simply is no margin of safety for investors entering today. If anything, they need flawless execution for years on end.

Autonomy and Robotics

Tesla's long-term potential story more and more depends on two pillars: autonomous driving and humanoid robotics. They both have transformational potential, but neither warrants the current valuation of the stock yet.

Autonomy-wise, Full Self-Driving (FSD) gets better, but real-world deployment continues to be held back by regulation, verification for safety, and hardware constraints.

Tesla's data advantage in this space is clear, with millions of vehicles collecting driving data on a daily basis, but the firm continues to operate in an overseen "Level 2+" paradigm. To move beyond that will require years of regulatory progress and liability resolution.

The same can be said of Tesla's humanoid robot project, Optimus. The tech demos are stunning, but commercialization is hypothetical. Commercial manufacture, working reliability, and safety regulators will arrive in due course, and the addressable market, which is obviously massive, is for now mostly theoretical.

These are fascinating optionality plays, but at the current share price, the market already prices in partial success in both. That is, even if Tesla succeeds, the upside is limited because so much of the "optionality going forward" is already priced into the multiple.

Energy and Storage

Tesla's energy generation and storage business unit has built up quietly as its second growth engine, with Megapack installations accelerating and quarterly energy revenue reaching more than $3 billion at times.

Over the long term, the business could be a big profit contributor as utilities transition to decarbonize and grid-scale storage demand accelerates.

However, it currently accounts for less than 10% of total revenue and comes on lower margins than the auto segment. Scaling it once more would require massive capital expenditures, manufacturing growth in Lathrop and Shanghai, and a consistent supply of lithium-ion batteries.

The opportunity is definitely real here, but at the current valuation, the market already anticipates a level of success that may take years to achieve.

Risks to the Bear Case

AI/Autonomy breakthrough: If Tesla achieves Level 4+ autonomy sooner than expected, valuation assumptions are reset. This is mainly due to the fact that the TAM is massive for those; this would change the world as we have it today forever.

Secondly, you have the energy storage scale-up: A sudden reversal in Megapack installations or grid contracts could de-risk earnings faster than anticipated.

Even accounting for these, risk-reward remains lopsided.

Conclusion

Tesla and Elon Musk in general deserve gigantic kudos for revolutionizing an industry, but marvelous businesses can be lousy stocks at the wrong price.

With earnings growth slowing, margin squeeze building, and the competition intensifying, today's valuation leaves little to chance. There are occasions when discipline in valuations trumps vision, and today is one such occasion.

For investors wanting good fundamentals at good multiples, plays like Molina Healthcare (MOH), Crocs (CROX), and even Sezzle (SEZL) now present much better asymmetric upside in this environment.

As such, I rated Tesla as a Strong Sell. Not because I don't believe in Tesla's future, but because valuation leaves me with no reason to believe it is a good investment for the long term at this price.

data.title: Boston Properties: Winner In The Office REITs Space
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on Boston Properties (NYSE:BXP). My summarized thesis is that the market continues to undervalue BXP despite clear, data-backed evidence that its fundamentals are inflecting. Leasing momentum is accelerating, the flight to quality in office demand is proving durable, and BXP is able to allocate more resources to drive portfolio occupancy up to 91% by 2027. With 90% of NOI derived from premier assets in supply-constrained markets and a new structural demand driver from AI-sector expansion, I believe BXP is in a good position to outperform.

Company overview

BXP is a fully integrated, self-administered real estate investment trust and is a developer, owner, and manager of premier workplaces in the United States. Its portfolio is concentrated in six key markets: Boston, Los Angeles, New York, San Francisco, Seattle, and Washington, DC. The BXP portfolio includes more than 180 properties, totaling more than 53 million square feet. It conducts substantially all of its business through its operating partnership, Boston Properties Limited Partnership, in which BXP holds ~89.6% ownership interest as of Q2 2025.

An Inflection Point in Occupancy

My belief is that BXP is at an inflection point of meaningful occupancy recovery, and I expect it to become the main driver for earnings growth over the next several years. Using management estimates, they expect in-service portfolio occupancy to improve from 86.4% in Q2 2025 to 89% by year-end 2026 and ~91% by year-end 2027. This ~500 bps improvement comes with a low hurdle, as only 4.7 million square feet are set to expire between mid-2025 and the end of 2027, which is a really small portion of the portfolio (BXP has ~54 million square feet of owned property). This level of churn means that BXP is able to focus on leasing activity to drive up occupancy rather than allocate resources to simply offset churn. This is not to say that BXP doesn’t need to address those 4.7 million square feet of lease expiry, but that a larger mix of sales reps can focus on finding growth opportunities (which drives up occupancy rate).

This isn’t just a theoretical path to higher earnings. The on-the-ground execution is already proving the thesis. As management noted, leasing activity today is “surprisingly robust compared to where it was 12 months ago.” We can see in the chart above, activity in H1 2025 was up ~22.2% y/y, and based on management comments, the pipeline is strong as well, with ~1.0 million square feet already signed in Q3 2025, plus ~1.1 million square feet under negotiation. All in all, this momentum signals improved demand and is especially bullish because it seems to be driven by secular, and not just cyclical, shifts. As President Douglas Linde said on the 2025 Investor Day conference:

To give you a perspective on sort of where a lot of the current vacancy in the portfolio is, it's sort of scattered, but it's -- actually, there's a reasonable amount in Manhattan in New York City. And then there's a heck of a lot in San Francisco. And so listening to what Rod and Christine have to say is going to be pretty important because that's where a lot of the ramp-up is going to come from as we move into '26 into 2027.

And then there's a reasonable amount of availability in our Urban Edge portfolio in Greater Waltham, and Pat and Bryan are going to talk about that, and they're going to give you sort of the granularity on where the leasing activity is today, and it's surprisingly robust compared to where it was 12 months ago. So that sort of gives you a perspective on where we're going to be from a "granular leasing" need as we move into 2026 and 2027.

Okay. So what does this all mean? How do you sort of translate this? So this is math. This is the way we are sort of looking at our portfolio today. So the average rent on the vacant and the expiring space, and we've sort of broken it into 3 portfolios. We have our East Coast portfolio, we have our West Coast portfolio, and then we have our suburban portfolio. That average is about $71 a square foot on a weighted basis.

I believe there is a “flight to quality” happening in the office space, and we can see this in the rental rates. My view is that tenants increasingly want quality spaces, those with high amenities and that are well-located, and this plays right into BXP’s DNA. For context, BXP has a long-standing strategy of owning and operating what it calls “premier workplaces,” essentially the top 15% of buildings in its markets. And in this case of tenants looking for quality spaces, this asset focus has become BXP's greatest competitive advantage.

To make it clear, this is not just BXP owning attractive assets; its implication goes beyond just that. With these assets, BXP is able to enjoy much better unit economics. The data illustrates this clearly. In BXP’s core CBD markets, premier assets command rents that are roughly 50% higher than non-premier peers, which reflects both pricing power and tenant preference. The difference doesn’t just surface in rental rates; vacancy in premier assets is just 12.5%, compared with ~20% across the rest of the market. This highlights how the best properties continue to attract and retain tenants even as broader office demand remains soft. Perhaps the most striking figure is net absorption: premier buildings have added 9.3 million square feet of leased space over the past two years, while non-premier buildings have lost 3.9 million square feet. Please note that Data in this paragraph source from the investor day presentation deck.

Given that BXP has 90.5% of its net operating income [NOI] coming from these top-tier CBD assets, BXP is definitely in a good position to benefit from this shift. What makes this even better than it already is is the near-total collapse of new office construction. Supply starts have fallen to effectively zero (source: investor day presentation slide), which means BXP’s existing portfolio now operates in an undersupply environment. And anyone with basic economics knowledge would know that growing demand paired with undersupply means pricing power.

Despite these data points, the market seems to, however, lump BXP together with generic “office” REITs, ignoring the fact that it operates in a distinctly healthier submarket with superior fundamentals. As leasing momentum converts into higher occupancy and NOI growth, I believe the market will re-rate BXP multiples higher.

Capturing the AI Growth Wave in Core Markets

One particularly interesting angle in BXP’s equity story is its growing exposure to the AI wave. Pretty much everyone knows there is an ongoing investment supercycle in the AI industry, and I think BXP is able to capitalize on this trend as well, with its geographic footprint and asset positioning.

As I mentioned above, BXP has a heavy presence in key cities, and one of them is the Bay Area. To put things in perspective, per the investor day presentation slide deck, this market has already captured an extraordinary 70% of all US AI venture funding since 2019. This statistic basically tells us how capital and talent are coming into BXP’s focused areas, and with such an influx of investment, it means there will be a surge in real estate demand. And if we look at the leasing activity (data also from the investor slide deck), in San Francisco alone, AI-related tenants have accounted for ~20–25% of all leasing activity over the past three years.

Note that these are well-capitalized, high-growth companies competing in an intense talent war (remember the Meta incident?), and as such, I would think having a premium, amenity-rich office space is table stakes (not a luxury). As such, BXP, being a provider of such premier workplaces, should really benefit from it.

Valuation

With all the visible tailwinds and tangible data points to prove that BXP is structurally well positioned to win demand vs. other normal office REITs, it was surprising to me that BXP is trading only at 21x NTM PE, which is the level that Corporate Office Properties (CDP) trades at, despite BXP growing earnings much faster (10% vs. low-single-digits for CDP). On a historical basis, BXP is also trading at a sharp discount vs. its 10-year average of 36x. I acknowledge that some discount is justified as the overall industry multiple has come down (because of the work-from-home impact and what not), but it shouldn’t be trading at the same multiple as CDP. Also, just a few months ago, BXP was trading at ~40x NTM PE. So, the market certainly has an appetite to re-rate BXP higher.

Looking ahead, I think the path for higher multiples lies on BXP showing the market that it can improve occupancy and translate that into earnings growth.

Investment Risk

Investors should be mindful of several key risks. A material downturn in the macro environment could dampen leasing activity and slow the pace of occupancy gains. My bullish thesis also depends on the stability of BXP’s blue-chip tenant base. Any sector-specific weakness could pressure rent collections or renewal rates. Additionally, with roughly 21% of NOI tied to joint ventures, there is an element of counterparty risk; if partners face financial strain, it could delay project timelines.

Conclusion

I recommend a buy rating for BXP. I like BXP’s exposure to premier assets, accelerating leasing momentum, and the emerging AI demand tailwind, all of which provide a credible pathway for sustainable earnings growth and multiple expansion. In my view, as occupancy steadily improves and secular trends continue to favor quality spaces, the market will begin to re-rate BXP’s multiple upwards.

data.title: Howard Hughes Deserves To Be On Everyones Watchlist
data.description:
Howard Hughes Holdings (NYSE: HHH) offers investors a unique opportunity to participate in Bill Ackman’s long-term compounding experiment. With Pershing Square now holding a 47% controlling stake and taking over management, HHH is transitioning from a cyclical land developer into a capital-allocation-driven holding company. The stock still trades like a real estate developer at roughly 10.5x forward free cash flow, but the new structure could justify a premium if Ackman successfully deploys capital into high-quality businesses and compounds intrinsic value over time. The key question is whether Pershing’s private equity style approach can create the kind of durable, compounding machine that made Berkshire Hathaway legendary.

Howard Hughes (HHH) has historically traded at a large discount to its net asset value (NAV) as a master planned community (MPC) developer, but its business model is changing after Pershing Square took a controlling interest in 2025. A Master Planned Community is a large-scale, mixed-use real estate development designed and built over many years. The company’s key assets include:

  • The Woodlands (Houston, TX)

  • Summerlin (Las Vegas, NV)

  • Bridgeland (Houston, TX)

  • Ward Village (Honolulu, HI)

  • Douglas Ranch (Phoenix, AZ)

HHH owns vast land plots, commercial and residential buildings, retail space, roads, and utilities. Its revenue model ($1.4bn sales LTM) combines recurring rental income from operating assets and cyclical land sales to homebuilders within its MPCs.

Over the last 15 years, Howard Hughes’s share price has moved largely sideways, frustrating long term holders including Bill Ackman who remains one of its most vocal investors (and now controlling shareholder). While the MPC model has strong intrinsic value, it’s been hampered by cyclicality tied to interest rates, land values, and housing/homebuilding demand. Howard Hughes Corporation (MPC business) is now a subsidiary of the Holdings Company which investors can buy shares in.

Pershing Square Takes Control

Bill Ackman’s Pershing Square now owns 47% of HHH, following a $900 million capital injection (10m shares @ $90 per share) and a new management services agreement (May 2025). Pershing’s, Ryan Israel, joins as CIO and as a board member, while Ackman returned as Executive Chairman.

Under the agreement, Pershing Square earns:

  • A fixed annual fee of $15 million (paid quarterly), and

  • A variable fee equal to 0.375% of HHH’s market cap above $3.93 billion, paid quarterly.

At a $7 billion market cap, Pershing would earn roughly $63 million annually, including base and variable fees. Over five years at that level, that translates to $305 million in cumulative payments. HHH gets access to Pershing investment expertise and famously impressive hedging abilities.

While this structure ensures alignment through growth incentives, one could argue it’s effectively a 1.5% annual management fee on market cap over $3.93bn, which could dilute shareholder value if HHH’s share price stagnates over many years.

Ackman’s Broader Strategy & Track record

Ackman has stated that his goal is to use HHH as a long-term holding company that acquires controlling stakes in durable, growing cash-generative businesses. This is a pivot from the traditional real estate-centric HHH.

Pershing Square’s portfolio includes Chipotle (CMG), Hilton (HLT), Restaurant Brands (QSR), Brookfield (BN), Uber (UBER), and Amazon (AMZN), positions that reflect a focus on quality compounding companies.

The new HHH could mirror that playbook, deploying its capital and borrowing capacity (or insurance float) to acquire smaller private companies or undervalued public firms, effectively functioning as a “mini-Berkshire” but with a private equity tilt (with the higher fee as well). Ackman recently told investors he's seeking to purchase an insurance company, that way HHH can utilise the float for acquisitions.

While Pershing Square has had major missteps (annotated in red below) most notably Valeant Pharmaceuticals which cost roughly $4 billion in losses, its overall track record is robust. Since 2004, Pershing Square’s NAV has compounded at roughly 16% annually, versus ~10% for the S&P 500.

Ackman looks for highly durable businesses that can both growth and maintain market share. Ackman’s resilience post Valeant/Herbal Life underscores his ability to pivot with later successes in Chipotle, Hilton, and Restaurant Brands generating multibagger returns. Of course Ackman's trades are often included in "some of the greatest trades on wall st" if you have a quick Google.

Fishing in the Small-Cap Pond

Ackman has repeatedly noted the valuation gap between small and large-cap equities. Below is a chart from Yardeni research showing historical forward P/E ratio's in small/mid/large caps and the mag-7.

The spread between large caps (blue line) and small/mid caps (red/green lines) diverged, breaking from its long-term trend over the last 5 years. This represents opportunity for HHH and Pershing Square to identify durable growing businesses and attractive valuations.

That 8x spread offers fertile ground for Pershing’s new acquisition strategy. With a team drawn from KKR, Apollo, Warburg Pincus, and Hellman & Friedman, Pershing’s investment team brings private equity expertise. They’re likely to take controlling stakes and actively shape portfolio company strategy rather than act as passive investors.

This contrasts sharply with Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), which historically lets its subsidiaries operate autonomously. Ackman’s model implies hands-on ownership, potentially blending the best (or worst) of private equity and public-market investing.

If Ackman follows his traditional approach he will aim find small businesses that are underperforming or undervalued but present great opportunity longer term as durable companies.

Fee Overhang

However calling this a mini-Berkshire could invite more criticism. Buffet's model, which many have struggled to copy, involved buying exceptional businesses over decades utilising Berkshire's insurance float avoiding debt and equity raises.

Importantly Berkshire, didn't charge fees (expect for his partnership). HHH have agreed to pay Pershing a fixed fee of $15m per year paid quarterly, plus a variable fee of 0.375% paid quarterly on the amount of market cap over $3.93bn.

Average Market Cap $5bn $6bn $7bn $8bn
Amount above reference ($3.93bn) $1.07bn $2.07 $3.07 $4.07
Quarterly Variable Fee 0.375% $4m $8m $12m $16m
Annualised $16m $32m $48m $64m
Fixed Fee $15m $15m $15m $15m
Total Annual Fees to Pershing $31m $47m $63m $79m

There is no one time payment, thus if HHH trades at $6bn in market cap and stays sideways in that range for 3 years - Pershing Square still earns circa $141m ($47m x 3) in fees over that period.

HHH, gets access to world leading investment advisory, however a better alignment mechanism might involve a “ratcheting” reference market cap, resetting each quarter once growth is achieved. I think this better incentives for long-term share price appreciation without dragging on the cash balance of HHH.

Risks

The biggest risk to the HHH thesis is execution. The success of this transformation depends on Pershing’s ability to source and integrate acquisitions that enhance earnings overtime. Meanwhile, the management fee structure introduces a recurring expense regardless of performance, potentially depressing free cash flow.

If Pershing Square pursues aggressive, leveraged acquisitions reminiscent of private equity (high leverage), it could expose HHH to balance sheet and integration risks. Ackman has vocalised his ambition to buy an insurance company, therefore he could be planning on utilising insurance float to partly or fully fund HHH acquisitions avoiding traditional leveraged PE-buyouts.

This approach would be lower risk than traditional PE investment styles because it doesn't require debt to fund buyouts which can increase the risk of bankruptcy when target companies underperforms. Ackman initially plans to buy stakes in smaller companies (not take privates) thus a prolonged small-cap bear market could delay or impair returns.

Conclusion

Since Pershing Square’s new management agreement was announced in May 2025, HHH shares have climbed over 20%, signalling positive sentiment from investors. Investors are betting that Pershing’s operational expertise and disciplined capital allocation will transform HHH from a cyclical land developer into a durable compounding platform.

However, this is not Berkshire 2.0, It’s an Ackman-engineered version more active, more fee-based. Pershing’s private equity style could accelerate growth, but its recurring management fees also risk constraining long-term compounding if value creation lags.

If Ackman can deploy HHH’s balance sheet effectively, achieve NAV growth of 15%+ annually and avoid overpaying for control, shareholders could enjoy strong returns. My gut tells me, Ackman has already found or at least has a few target companies in mind and HHH could be transacting in H1 of 2026.

data.title: U.S. Economy: A Storm Is Coming
data.description:
I have lived in South Florida for more than a quarter of a century now. During the summer months, Floridians always have their eyes cast southeastward, monitoring potential storms brewing off North Africa and in the Caribbean. Fortunately, this has been one of the lightest hurricane seasons in recent memory, and no major storms have hit our shores during this season.

However, watching what is going on in the stock market, it seems apparent that a storm is brewing. For now, it remains offshore but has substantial potential to develop into a major hurricane. If and when it hits shore can only be speculated about at the moment. At what magnitude and where it hits is also in question.

However, it is difficult to argue that equities are not severely overbought on a historical basis, looking at the market through traditional valuation metrics. In addition, the economy has major issues that investors are content presently overlooking. Insiders appear to know that the market is overvalued, given the insider buy-to-sell ratio hovers near its lowest level in a half decade.

In today's column, I look at three core things that could trigger the next potential storm in the markets and push the U.S. economy into a recession.

Sovereign Debt:

The federal debt is like the weather. Everybody talks about it, but nobody does anything about it. However, this situation can only go on for so long before it can become problematic as the debt-to-GDP ratio hovers near the highest levels in American history.

Concerns are increasing around the U.S. government running an approximate $2 trillion annual deficit. This is one reason the dollar declined by nearly 11% against other currencies in the first half of this year, the weakest six months for the greenback to start a year since 1973. Gold has also gone somewhat parabolic this year and just surged past $4,000 an ounce for the first time.

However, the United States is hardly the only nation with a significant sovereign debt issue. The debt-to-GDP ratio in Japan is north of 230%, and the country has the second-oldest population on the planet. Including regional governmental debt, China has a debt-to-GDP ratio of approximately 300%.

Things aren't much better in Europe. The fifth prime minister in two years in France just stepped down as the country remains unable to pass a budget. It is hardly the only European nation struggling with debt issues right now, it should be noted.

At what point this massive debt load becomes a problem is uncertain. However, when this issue finally bubbles up to the surface, it will likely cause a major problem for the global economy and the markets, akin to at least the European Debt Crisis that started in 2009 and lingered for years.

Struggling Consumers:

The majority of American households are struggling economically. This is not reflected in some readings like retail sales, simply because the top 10% of American households (roughly those with $250,000 or more in annual income) are doing well and are responsible for roughly 50% of all consumer spending. Thirty years ago, they drove just over 35% of overall consumer spending. However, these struggles are captured by dismal consumer sentiment readings.

The average American household lost buying power from 2021-2024 thanks to the highest inflation levels since the late 1970s/early 1980s.

Given this, it is not surprising that consumer loan delinquencies are on the rise. Outside of prime borrowers, delinquencies in auto loans are at Great Financial Crisis levels across several metrics. There is just over $1.6 trillion of auto loans outstanding in the United States.

Student loans are becoming a slow-moving train wreck after payments and collection activities were restarted earlier this year after a nearly four-year taxpayer-funded hiatus. As of July, 29% of federal student loan borrowers are 90 or more days past due, according to TransUnion. There is nearly $1.8 trillion of student loan debt outstanding, with just over 40 million Americans with outstanding loan balances. Defaulted student loans will increasingly lead to a surge of wage garnishments in the months ahead. Tax refunds and other federal payouts can also be grabbed by the government to settle arrears.

Credit card delinquency and charge-off rates are on the rise again, and credit card debt stands at a record $1.21 trillion.

Job Market:

There is a total disconnect right now between what is going on with the stock market that continues to rally from one new all-time high to another this year, and what is a clearly faltering job market.

Before the BLS quit issuing reports due to the government shutdown, the government agency slashed its previous projections for March 2024 to March 2025 job growth by 919,000 positions. The August BLS report showed only 22,000 jobs created for the month, below expectations of 75,000. The June and July BLS jobs numbers were also revised down sharply as well.

Last week, the ADP jobs report showed 32,000 jobs lost in September. The government shutdown prevented the BLS from issuing its September jobs reading as scheduled last Friday. Investment firm Carlyle did their own analysis showing only 17,000 jobs were created last month.

The scary thing about this job market slowdown is twofold. First, equities continue to rally even as this key economic engine is sputtering. Second, the impact from increasing AI capabilities is just starting to displace workers and could potentially trigger a White-Collar Recession.

I think Atlanta Fed President Bostic had a good comment on this developing trend earlier this week, my emphasis added.

More businesses are seeing generative AI as a complement to their current labor force. However, when companies see an opportunity to replace workers with AI, they do take it.

So, the country faces unprecedented debt levels. The majority of consumers are struggling on myriad fronts, including rising loan delinquencies and weakening job prospects. To me, this constitutes a growing storm on the horizon.

When this storm hits our shores and its impact is unknown. That is why my portfolio is roughly 25% in short-term Treasuries, with almost all the rest of the allocation within covered call holdings around the few stocks I am finding in the market sporting reasonable valuations.

data.title: Bain Capital Specialty Finance: P/NAV Of 0.78x, Double-Digit Yield; This BDC Is A Hidden Gem (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

As someone whose primary investment objective is income, the BDC (BIZD) sector is one I watch closely. I sold out of all my BDC holdings earlier this year due to anticipated headwinds from lower base rates and increased economic volatility. But many are much more attractive now that base rates have come down.

With more rate cuts anticipated in the coming months, investors expect more volatility. And while the sector has experienced a pullback over the past 10 months, many BDCs could be worth dollar-cost averaging into at current levels.

A BDC that currently fits the bill is Bain Capital Specialty Finance (NYSE:BCSF). And as a result of their discount to NAV and strong fundamentals, BCSF is one of the few BDCs I think could be worth nibbling on at the current price.

In this article I discuss their latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I'm downgrading them from a buy to a hold.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Bain Capital Specialty Finance in January of this year, assigning the BDC a buy rating due to their strong credit quality, impressive earnings, and discount to its net asset value.

As one of the few smaller-sized BDCs I considered to be of high quality, BCSF's discount to its peers was compelling for income-focused investors. Despite this, their smaller size, increased economic volatility, and lower interest rates posed risks, something I cautioned investors about going forward.

Since, BCSF has seen its share price slide, down nearly 21% in the past nine months, while the S&P (SP500) has continued its strong performance, up 12.23%.

Solid Credit Quality Amidst Headwinds

Since my last coverage on BCSF, the BDC has reported earnings three times, their last earnings report this past August. As a result, mounting headwinds have been evident from lower base rates, as well as a tough economic backdrop.
